title: Lipstick on Cotton
stainChemistry: Lipstick is a waxy dye matrix: beeswax / carnauba / candelilla wax (the structural frame), oils (castor oil, lanolin, mineral oil), pigments (D&C Red 6, Red 7, Red 21, plus iron oxides for opacity), and emollients. The wax sets the shape; the oils carry the pigment into the fiber when the lipstick smears. Cotton tolerates IPA, POG, NSD, and 3% H₂O₂ at room temperature. The sequence is solvent-first: dissolve the wax-oil carrier, transfer the dye onto a catch cloth, emulsify residual oil with detergent, oxidize faint shadow if needed.
whyThisWorks: Water won't touch lipstick — wax and oil are hydrophobic, so soap alone spreads the pigment without removing it. IPA or POG (pro) / rubbing alcohol (home) breaks up the wax + oil matrix in one pass. With the wax dissolved, the dye transfers into a catch cloth instead of smearing sideways. NSD / Dawn cleans the residual oil film. 3% H₂O₂ clears faint dye shadow on whites.
defaultAssumption: Fresh lipstick on cotton (collar, cuff, pocket) is a high-confidence DIY. Aged or washed-in lipstick needs 2–3 solvent passes but usually stays in home scope. Heat-set (through a dryer) trends toward escalation.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull blade, clean white cotton towel as catch cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape solid lipstick off with a dull blade edge — don't rub. Lift the waxy bulk before adding any solvent. Then place a folded white catch cloth UNDER the stain so the dissolved dye transfers downward, not sideways into clean fabric.

spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent on cloth, tamp from back
spottingProtocol.equipment: Isopropyl alcohol 90%+ OR POG on clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply the solvent to a clean white cloth. Tamp from the BACK of the fabric, over the catch cloth. Flip the pressing cloth frequently — the dye transfers onto whichever surface it's pressed against. 2–3 passes usually clear fresh lipstick; deeper/aged stains need more. Never pour solvent directly on cotton.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. NSD emulsifies the residual oil film after the solvent pass. Rinse cool from the back.
For residual dye shadow on whites: 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Oxidizes the dye color. Dwell 3–5 min, blot. Skip on dark or dyed cotton. One oxidizer at a time.
Rinse. Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer — dryer heat sets any residual dye permanently.

steps: 1. Scrape waxy bulk. Place catch cloth UNDER the stain.
2. IPA or POG on a white cloth, tamp from the back. Flip cloth. 2–3 passes.
4. 3% H₂O₂ on residual dye shadow (whites only). 3–5 min.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Solvent before water — water spreads waxy pigment without removing it
Always tamp with a catch cloth under + pressing cloth on top
One oxidizer at a time; skip peroxide on dark/dyed cotton

homeSolutions.instruction: Use a dull butter knife or credit-card edge to lift any solid lipstick off the fabric. Don't rub. The wax has structure — scraping lifts it cleanly; rubbing grinds the pigment into the weave.

homeSolutions.agent: Put a White Towel UNDER the Stain
homeSolutions.instruction: Fold a clean white towel and place it UNDER the stained area. When the solvent dissolves the wax, the dye transfers downward into the catch cloth instead of smearing sideways into clean fabric. This is the step that separates 'stain gone' from 'stain bigger.'

homeSolutions.agent: Rubbing Alcohol on a Clean Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab 70%+ rubbing alcohol (drugstore isopropyl) on a clean white cloth. Press the cloth onto the stain from the BACK of the fabric. Flip the pressing cloth to a clean spot every few seconds — the dye transfers onto whichever cloth is touching it. 2–3 passes usually clears fresh lipstick. Cotton handles alcohol fine.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Soft Brush for the Oil
homeSolutions.instruction: Work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the spot with a soft toothbrush. Dawn breaks up the oil film the solvent pass left behind. Rinse cool.

homeSolutions.agent: Hydrogen Peroxide 3% for Residual Shadow (Whites Only)
homeSolutions.instruction: For a residual pink or red shadow on white cotton: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the spot, let it bubble 3–5 min, blot. Skip on dark or dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia — toxic fume risk.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. Any residual lipstick sets permanently under dryer heat. If you still see color, repeat the alcohol + Dawn pass.

If the lipstick has been hot-dried, or two full home rounds haven't cleared it, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Lipstick on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with rubbing alcohol + Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide. [Hot-washed / not yet laundered.]"

steps: 1. Scrape waxy lipstick bulk off with a dull blade — don't rub.
2. Place a clean white towel UNDER the stain as a catch cloth.
3. Rubbing alcohol on a white cloth, tamp from the BACK. Flip cloth. 2–3 passes.
4. Dawn + soft toothbrush for residual oil.
5. 3% hydrogen peroxide on residual shadow (whites only).
6. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
7. Escalate if heat-set or two rounds failed.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if lipstick has been through a hot dryer, if two home rounds haven't cleared it, or if the cotton is dark dyed and peroxide isn't safe.
warnings: Solvent before water — water alone spreads waxy pigment
Never pour alcohol on fabric — tamp with cloth, catch cloth underneath
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia

neverDo: Never skip the catch cloth — sideways dye spread is the #1 home failure
Never pour solvent — tamp with a cloth
Never rub wet lipstick — smears the wax matrix into fresh fiber
Never run residual lipstick through a hot dryer
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates IPA, POG, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, and hot wash
Dyed cotton: alcohol is usually safe; test inside seam if concerned about fabric dye
Heat-durable fiber — hot wash is the finisher

materialWarnings: Solvent first, water after — water alone doesn't touch wax
Catch cloth UNDER + tamp on TOP — always
Inspect before the dryer — heat sets residual dye

escalation.when: Lipstick heat-set by iron or hot dryer, two home rounds failed, or dark dyed cotton where peroxide isn't safe and alcohol alone plateaus.
escalation.whatToTell: "Lipstick on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with rubbing alcohol + Dawn + 3% H₂O₂. [Hot-washed / not yet laundered.]"

contextQuestions: Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Long-wear / matte lipstick or traditional cream? (matte/long-wear has more polymer, needs more solvent passes)
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Most home attempts stall on lipstick because they start with soap + water. The wax-and-oil carrier beads off water; solvent is the unlock. Catch cloth under + pressing cloth on top is the second non-negotiable — without it, the dissolved dye smears sideways.
